data.title: National Fuel Gas (NFG) EPS Rebound Reinforces Bullish Valuation Narrative
data.description:
National Fuel Gas (NFG) opened fiscal Q1 2026 with total revenue of US$651.5 million and basic EPS of US$1.99, alongside net income excluding extra items of US$181.6 million. The company has seen quarterly revenue move from US$549.5 million and EPS of US$0.50 in Q1 2025 to US$651.5 million and EPS of US$1.99 in Q1 2026. Trailing twelve month EPS sits at US$7.23 on revenue of about US$2.4 billion, placing margins and earnings quality in focus for investors assessing the latest results.

See our full analysis for National Fuel Gas.

With the headline numbers on the table, the next step is to see how this earnings print lines up against the widely held narratives around National Fuel Gas's growth profile, risk factors, and income appeal.

Curious how numbers become stories that shape markets? Explore Community Narratives

US$2.4b trailing revenue sets the backdrop

  • Over the last twelve months, National Fuel Gas generated about US$2.4b in revenue and US$655.2 million in net income excluding extra items, with trailing EPS of US$7.23 giving you a fuller picture than any single quarter.
  • What stands out in the general market view is the mix of stability and cyclicality, and the trailing figures help you see that balance in practice:
    • Compared with Q1 2025, where net income excluding extra items was US$45.0 million, the latest Q1 print of US$181.6 million sits within a much stronger trailing earnings base of US$655.2 million.
    • The trailing EPS path from US$0.84 in Q4 2024 to US$7.23 by Q1 2026 highlights that the recent recovery is spread across several quarters, not just one strong period.
To see how these numbers fit into a bigger story investors are debating, check out the full narrative that has been built around them: 📊 Read the full National Fuel Gas Consensus Narrative.

P/E of 12.2x versus peers at 14.7x and 19x

  • The trailing P/E of 12.2x sits below the Global Gas Utilities industry average of 14.7x and a peer average of 19x, while the shares are also cited as around 33.1% below a stated fair value estimate.
  • Bulls argue that this kind of discount is hard to ignore, and the recent profit record is part of their case:
    • Earnings are recorded as having grown about 6.3% per year over the last five years, with the company now profitable on US$655.2 million of trailing net income excluding extra items. This aligns with the view that investors are not paying a high multiple for that earnings base.
    • At the same time, revenue is forecast to grow around 11.4% per year and earnings about 9% per year. From a bullish perspective, a 12.2x P/E and a share price of US$84 are seen as not fully reflecting those growth figures, especially when the DCF fair value is cited at US$125.54.

Debt load and dividend coverage in focus

  • The stock offers a 2.55% dividend yield, but that dividend is flagged as not well covered by free cash flow and the company is described as carrying a high level of debt.
  • Skeptics highlight these balance sheet and cash flow points as key counterweights to the more optimistic story:
    • Even with US$655.2 million in trailing net income excluding extra items, the fact that free cash flow coverage of the dividend is weak means income investors may want to look closely at how comfortably that 2.55% yield is supported.
    • The mention of a high level of debt, alongside ongoing investment needs across utility, pipeline, and upstream operations, is cited by bears as a reason to question how much flexibility the company has if conditions become less favorable.

data.title: AstraZeneca’s China Bet Recasts Growth Story Around Obesity And Diabetes
data.description:
• AstraZeneca (LSE:AZN) has announced a $15b investment in China focused on R&D and manufacturing.
  • The plan includes building end to end cell therapy capabilities and expanding local partnerships with Chinese and UK institutions.
  • The company has also agreed a new partnership with CSPC Pharmaceuticals to develop obesity and diabetes treatments using AI driven discovery and once monthly dosing platforms.

AstraZeneca, a global biopharmaceutical group focused on oncology, cardiovascular, renal, metabolism and respiratory diseases, is tying this China expansion directly to its core therapeutic areas. Obesity and diabetes are major focus points for healthcare systems, and large drug makers are competing to build pipelines around next generation treatments. For investors, these developments sit within a broader push across the sector toward targeted therapies, AI supported drug discovery and more convenient dosing schedules.

For you as a shareholder or potential investor, the key questions are how this $15b commitment and the CSPC partnership might reshape AstraZeneca's risk profile, capital allocation and future product mix. The scale and scope of the China plan, along with the focus on obesity and diabetes, could influence how the market views LSE:AZN's growth drivers, regional exposure and dependence on future regulatory and clinical milestones.

Stay updated on the most important news stories for AstraZeneca by adding it to your watchlist or portfolio. Alternatively, explore our Community to discover new perspectives on AstraZeneca.

How AstraZeneca stacks up against its biggest competitors

AstraZeneca's planned US$15b commitment in China, paired with the US$1.2b upfront CSPC deal for obesity and diabetes drugs, points to a heavier tilt toward chronic metabolic diseases and China-sourced R&D capacity. It also locks in once monthly dosing technology that could appeal to patients weighing options across competing GLP 1 class therapies from Novo Nordisk and Eli Lilly. For you, the key takeaway is that capital and pipeline are being directed toward areas where large-cap pharma is already very active, so execution, differentiation on dosing, safety and access, and how quickly these assets move beyond Phase I will be important to watch.

Advertisement

AstraZeneca narrative, now with a China and obesity twist

These moves sit squarely inside the existing AstraZeneca narratives that focus on oncology strength, emerging market expansion and investment in next generation platforms. They extend the cardiometabolic side of the story and deepen China as a core geography rather than just an add-on growth market. For investors who follow the long-term 2030 revenue ambitions discussed in those narratives, this China and CSPC push can be seen as part of the company's effort to broaden growth drivers beyond a handful of blockbuster oncology brands, while still leaning on partnerships and external platforms instead of relying only on in-house R&D.

Risks and rewards to keep in mind

  • 🎁 Access to eight obesity and diabetes programmes and an established once monthly platform could strengthen AstraZeneca's cardiometabolic portfolio at a time when long acting GLP 1 based drugs are a priority for many healthcare systems.
  • 🎁 The China build out across cell therapy, radioconjugates and manufacturing, plus broader academic and biotech partnerships, may widen AstraZeneca's option set for future products in oncology and autoimmune disease.
  • ⚠️ The large up front and milestone commitments to CSPC, together with US$15b of China capex, raise execution and return on capital questions, particularly if clinical readouts or regulatory timelines do not progress as planned.
  • ⚠️ Heavier exposure to China and weight management drugs adds policy, pricing and competitive risks, especially as governments push on drug costs and as peers like Novo Nordisk and Eli Lilly advance their own obesity and diabetes franchises.

What to watch next

From here, it makes sense to track how quickly SYH2082 and the other CSPC programmes clear Phase I, how regulators respond to once monthly metabolic drugs, and whether AstraZeneca's China facilities start to feature more prominently in global product announcements relative to peers such as Pfizer and Merck. If you want a broader sense of how this fits with oncology, US manufacturing plans and long term growth debates, check community narratives for AstraZeneca on its company page.

  • Supporters of a more bullish view often focus on how this kind of earnings track record can back up a quality story, yet the data itself adds some balance:
    • Trailing net margin of 18.1% compared with 12.8% a year earlier ties in with that quality angle, as it points to a larger share of each krone of revenue dropping to the bottom line.

data.title: Optiscan Imaging's Recent Gains Improve Losses On Insider Purchases Worth AU$1.38m
data.description:
Insiders who bought AU$1.38m worth of Optiscan Imaging Limited (ASX:OIL) stock in the last year have seen some of their losses recouped as the stock gained 10% last week. However, total losses seen by insiders are still AU$490k since the time of purchase.

While insider transactions are not the most important thing when it comes to long-term investing, logic dictates you should pay some attention to whether insiders are buying or selling shares.

This technology could replace computers: discover the 20 stocks are working to make quantum computing a reality.

Advertisement

The Last 12 Months Of Insider Transactions At Optiscan Imaging

Over the last year, we can see that the biggest insider purchase was by insider Robert Peters for AU$1.3m worth of shares, at about AU$0.15 per share. That means that even when the share price was higher than AU$0.096 (the recent price), an insider wanted to purchase shares. Their view may have changed since then, but at least it shows they felt optimistic at the time. To us, it's very important to consider the price insiders pay for shares. Generally speaking, it catches our eye when insiders have purchased shares at above current prices, as it suggests they believed the shares were worth buying, even at a higher price.

While Optiscan Imaging insiders bought shares during the last year, they didn't sell. You can see the insider transactions (by companies and individuals) over the last year depicted in the chart below. By clicking on the graph below, you can see the precise details of each insider transaction!

See our latest analysis for Optiscan Imaging

There are always plenty of stocks that insiders are buying. If investing in lesser known companies is your style, you could take a look at this free list of companies. (Hint: insiders have been buying them).

Insiders At Optiscan Imaging Have Bought Stock Recently

There was some insider buying at Optiscan Imaging over the last quarter. Non-Executive Director Karen Borg shelled out AU$18k for shares in that time. We like it when there are only buyers, and no sellers. But the amount invested in the last three months isn't enough for us too put much weight on it, as a single factor.

Does Optiscan Imaging Boast High Insider Ownership?

Many investors like to check how much of a company is owned by insiders. A high insider ownership often makes company leadership more mindful of shareholder interests. Insiders own 27% of Optiscan Imaging shares, worth about AU$27m. While this is a strong but not outstanding level of insider ownership, it's enough to indicate some alignment between management and smaller shareholders.

So What Does This Data Suggest About Optiscan Imaging Insiders?

Our data shows a little insider buying, but no selling, in the last three months. Overall the buying isn't worth writing home about. On a brighter note, the transactions over the last year are encouraging. Overall we don't see anything to make us think Optiscan Imaging insiders are doubting the company, and they do own shares. So while it's helpful to know what insiders are doing in terms of buying or selling, it's also helpful to know the risks that a particular company is facing.
